did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (logistic regression on lesion features and patient metadata) and deep learning (Convolutional Neural Networks/CNNs analyzing dermoscopic images) to diagnose atypical pigmented facial lesions (aPFLs) and differentiate between malignant and benign skin cancers.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a retrospective, multicentric study involving the analysis of 1197 dermoscopic images. It describes specific methods (logistic regression and CNN models), datasets (iDScore project), and quantitative results (precision, sensitivity, specificity, accuracy metrics) comparing AI models for diagnosing atypical pigmented facial lesions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Comparative Analysis of AI Models for Atypical Pigmented Facial Lesion Diagnosis
brief_summary: This study evaluated and compared the diagnostic effectiveness of machine learning (logistic regression) and deep learning (convolutional neural network) models for atypical pigmented facial lesions (aPFLs). The research utilized 1197 dermoscopic images from the iDScore project, classified into seven categories including lentigo maligna and melanoma. The logistic regression model achieved 100% sensitivity but low specificity (33.9%), while the CNN model demonstrated higher specificity (90.8%) and accuracy (59.5%) for melanoma diagnosis. Although dermatologists showed higher accuracy in binary malignant/benign differentiation (71.2%) than in specific diagnosis (42.9%), the AI models provided valuable support. The authors conclude that while CNNs offer objective future potential, logistic regression is currently more practical for immediate clinical use due to data limitations.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant lesion; 503 images (41.7% of dataset); grouped with LMM for logistic regression binary outcome and CNN diagnosis.
Malignant lesion; grouped with LM for logistic regression binary outcome and CNN diagnosis.

all_task_types.target: Diagnostic category of atypical pigmented facial lesions (aPFLs)
all_task_types.details: The study performs two classification tasks: 1) Binary classification of malignant (LM + LMM) vs. benign lesions using a logistic regression model based on clinical/dermoscopic features and a CNN model based on dermoscopic images. 2) Multiclass classification into seven specific diagnostic categories (LM, LMM, AN, PAK, SL, SK, SLK) using the CNN model. The logistic regression output is a discrete score (0-16) used to classify lesions into malignant/benign ranges, not a continuous regression target.

datasets_used.name: iDScore-Facial Project Dataset
datasets_used.description: A retrospective, multicentric collection of dermoscopic images from twelve European centers (Universities of Siena, Modena, Reggio Emilia, Napoli, Bologna, Aviano, Trieste, Thessaloniki, Nis, Saint Etienne, Brussels, and Gothenburg) as part of the Integrated Dermoscopy Score (iDScore) project. The dataset consists of histologically confirmed facial lesions excised due to suspicion of malignancy.

use.training: Used for training the logistic regression model (80% of dataset via 5-fold cross-validation) and fine-tuning the CNN model (combined with ISIC 2018 images).
use.validation: Used for validation of the logistic regression model (via 5-fold cross-validation) and the CNN model (via 5-fold cross-validation).
use.testing: Used for testing the logistic regression model (remaining 20% of dataset) and the CNN model (111 unseen images from this dataset).
use.other: Used for dermatologist evaluation (2445 evaluations by 154 dermatologists).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018 Dataset
datasets_used.description: Images extracted from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 dataset, specifically facial atypical pigmented facial lesions (aPFLs).

use.training: Combined with the iDScore dataset (1197 images) for fine-tuning the CNN model.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To select predictor variables for the diagnostic model based on their contribution to the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC).

statistical_methods_used.method: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used as the criterion for variable selection in the stepwise logistic regression (threshold of 0.003 contribution) and to evaluate model performance.
Used for training and validating both the logistic regression model and the CNN model to optimize performance and select the best model.
To visualize and compare the diagnostic performance of the AI models and dermatologists.
To display the classification performance of the CNN model on the testing sample.
Used as the loss function for training the CNN model to handle class imbalance.
Used as the optimizer for training the CNN model.
Used as the learning rate scheduler for the CNN model (factor = 0.1, patience = 3, threshold = 0.0001).
To manage overfitting in the CNN model by stopping training if validation loss did not decrease by at least 0.03 within 10 epochs.
To summarize patient demographics (age) and dataset distribution (gender, lesion types).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests) for comparing model performances. The logistic regression variable selection used a threshold based on AUROC contribution (0.003) and statistical significance, but specific p-values or formal hypothesis test results for the final model comparisons are not provided.

iDScore-Facial Project Dataset (Testing.notes: The logistic regression model was tested on the remaining 20% of the iDScore dataset. A cut-off value of 3 was chosen to maximize sensitivity. The model score ranges from 0 to 16. Ranges defined: Very low (0-2), Intermediate (3-9), Very high (10-16).
The CNN model was tested on 111 unseen images from the iDScore dataset. It was trained on iDScore images combined with ISIC 2018 images.
Comparison of sensitivity between the CNN model and dermatologists on the testing set.

iDScore-Facial Project Dataset (Dermatol.notes: Accuracy of dermatologists' direct diagnosis performance across different grouping levels of the seven diagnostic categories.

ISIC 2018 Dataset.notes: 743 images from the ISIC 2018 dataset were used for fine-tuning the CNN model alongside the 1197 iDScore images. No specific test results are reported for ISIC 2018 alone; it served as part of the training/fine-tuning data.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained model fine-tuned on 1197 iDScore images and 743 images from the ISIC 2018 dataset.

architecture_details.rationale: Chosen over more complex models (ResNet-101, EfficientNet B0, EfficientNet B1) which yielded similar or worse results.
architecture_details.implementation: Python v3.8 and PyTorch framework v2.2.0

training_details.learning_rate_scheduler: Reduce learning rate on plateau (factor = 0.1, patience = 3, threshold = 0.0001)
training_details.early_stopping: Stopped if validation loss did not decrease by at least 0.03 within 10 epochs
data_augmentation: Color transformations (brightness, contrast, saturation with probability = 0.1)

classification_or_output_head.note: LM and LMM diagnoses were aggregated for binary classification tasks.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied during CNN training: crop (probability = 0.1), horizontal flip (probability = 0.5), vertical flip (probability = 0.5), and color transformations (brightness, contrast, and saturation with probability = 0.1).

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Brightness, contrast, and saturation transformations applied with probability = 0.1

conclusions: The logistic regression model, which integrates clinical data and dermoscopic features, achieved 100% sensitivity but low specificity (33.9%), making it a practical and simple decision support system for immediate clinical use, particularly for ruling out malignancy (score 0-2) or recommending biopsy (score >10).

The CNN model (ResNet-34) demonstrated higher specificity (90.8%) and accuracy (59.5%) for melanoma diagnosis compared to the logistic regression model, with a diagnostic sensitivity about 23% higher than that of dermatologists.
Dermatologists showed higher accuracy in binary malignant/benign differentiation (71.2%) than in distinguishing between seven specific diagnostic categories (42.9%).

The CNN model requires extensive data to achieve clinic-ready accuracy, whereas the logistic regression model is more practical for immediate clinical use with smaller datasets, though it relies on subjective feature identification by physicians.
The CNN model represents the future direction of AI in dermatology by offering an objective evaluation of lesions with the potential for greater accuracy as more data become available.

AI models can enhance diagnostic accuracy in complex dermatological cases like atypical pigmented facial lesions by integrating with clinical data, potentially saving biopsies for benign lesions and improving the detection of malignant ones.
important_remarks: The CNN model was trained on a relatively small dataset (1197 iDScore images + 743 ISIC 2018 images), which is a significant limitation for deep learning models that typically require larger datasets for optimal performance.

The logistic regression model's performance is heavily dependent on the subjective identification of dermoscopic features by the enrolled dermatologists, introducing potential bias from physician expertise and assessment variability.
The study lacks external validation; the dataset was collected from twelve specific European centers, limiting generalizability to other populations or clinical settings.

The logistic regression model achieved 100% sensitivity but very low specificity (33.9%), indicating a high rate of false positives and potential for unnecessary biopsies if used without clinical context.

The CNN model demonstrated lower sensitivity (58.2%) for the malignant class (LM + LMM) compared to dermatologists (55.5% sensitivity, but note the CNN's higher specificity of 90.8% vs LR's 33.9%), highlighting the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity in AI models.

The dataset is imbalanced, with malignant lesions (LM + LMM) comprising only 41.7% of the sample, and rare classes like Seborrheic Lichenoid Keratosis (SLK) having only 50 images, which may affect model robustness for minority classes.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported for comparing the performance of AI models against dermatologists, limiting the statistical rigor of the comparative claims.
The logistic regression model relies on a score cut-off (0-16) derived from the training data, which may not be universally applicable without recalibration in different clinical populations.
The study is retrospective, which may introduce selection bias as lesions were excised due to suspicion of malignancy, potentially overrepresenting malignant cases compared to a screening population.
The CNN model's performance was evaluated on a small test set of 111 unseen images from the iDScore dataset, which may not provide a robust estimate of its real-world diagnostic accuracy.
